title: Emcure eyes India growth, global deals amid strong demand
summary: Emcure Pharmaceuticals is boosting domestic expansion, R&D, and manufacturing. The company is also exploring acquisition opportunities in India and internationally. New products are expected to drive significant growth. Emcure's partnership with Novo Nordisk...
thbAction: Use the article as an entry point to pitch THB's commercial visibility layer for territory planning, account prioritization, and next-best-action for pharma leadership teams.
contentHtml:
Synopsis

Emcure Pharmaceuticals is boosting domestic expansion, R&D, and manufacturing. The company is also exploring acquisition opportunities in India and internationally. New products are expected to drive significant growth. Emcure's partnership with Novo Nordisk for GLP-1 therapies and a pipeline of complex injectables will support future momentum.

Emcure Pharmaceuticals is planning to step up investments in domestic expansion, R&D and manufacturing, even as it scans for acquisition opportunities, top company officials told ET.

“A lot of our capacities on the manufacturing side are filling up. It's a good problem to have because it's been faster than expected. There are a couple of additions that we are making in terms of injectable lines and some facilities. …And eyes, ears always open, not only in India, but also internationally, for any good M&A opportunities that come up, whether in a specific therapy, a specific brand, or in some of the markets like Europe or Canada,” Samit Mehta, whole-time director, said.

“Domestic is very close to my heart. So, if you get some more opportunity in India, why not, that will be a priority… as we keep on looking for bolt-on acquisitions in other geographies,” Managing director & CEO Satish Mehta said.

New products are expected to contribute meaningfully to growth. “I would see it in double digits, early mid-teens kind of contribution from new products in the next five years,” Samit Mehta added.

On Tuesday, the Pune-based pharma company reported 17% year-over-year growth in FY27 revenue to Rs 9,204 crore, while profit after tax stood at Rs 941 crore, up 33%. Full year EBITDA was Rs 1,789 crore, up 21.8% YoY, with EBITDA margins at 19.4%. For the March quarter, the company posted revenue of Rs 2,470 crore, up 17% and PAT of Rs 244 crore, up 24%, reflecting steady operational momentum.

Satish Mehta said the company’s partnership with Novo Nordisk in GLP-1 therapies will be one of the key growth drivers, while international momentum will be supported by a pipeline of complex injectables.

Emcure has a marketing tie-up with the Danish innovator Novo Nordisk to distribute and commercialise another brand of semaglutide under the name Poviztra.

The company also plans to sharpen its domestic focus despite strong international traction. “We want to double down on the domestic market and have a better growth than what we have achieved in the year which has ended,” Mehta said.

The company is also betting heavily on complex generics and biosimilars. “On the complex injectables, it is going to be driven by more products of the liposomal platform… we’ve been able to demonstrate proof of concept,” said Samit Mehta.

He added that existing biosimilars remain under-penetrated: “Each of them is a goldmine and we have barely scratched the surface.”

On outlook, the company remains cautiously optimistic. “For FY27, we do expect low to mid-teens growth… and our endeavour is to improve margins every year,” Piyush Nahar, EVP – corporate development and strategy said.

While geopolitical tensions could push up input costs, the company said it remains insulated in the near term. “We do keep between one to two quarters of inventory… so we are shielded for some time,” Samit Mehta said.
